---
title: "Capability Replacement and Rollback"
chapter_id: "capability-replacement-and-rollback"
part_id: "foundations-alignment-governance"
status: "conceptual"
draft_maturity: "v0.2 manuscript draft"
last_updated: "2026-06-24"
primary_sources:
  - "scf"
  - "rmi"
  - "benchmaxxing"
  - "cognitive_loop_closure"
  - "talos"
  - "moecot"
evidence_level: "argument"
claim_label: "Design rationale"
open_evidence_gaps:
  - "Chapter is a v0.2 manuscript draft; v1.0 still needs source-note substantiation for all assigned sources."
  - "No Codex tests have been implemented or run for this chapter unless separately recorded in Appendix E."
  - "No support-state promotion is implied by this drafting pass."
---

# Capability Replacement and Rollback

## Chapter status

| Field | Value |
|---|---|
| Chapter ID | `capability-replacement-and-rollback` |
| Part | Part I - Foundations, Alignment, and Governance |
| Status | conceptual |
| Manuscript maturity | v0.2 manuscript draft |
| Last updated | 2026-06-24 |
| Primary source records | `scf`, `rmi`, `benchmaxxing`, `cognitive_loop_closure`, `talos`, `moecot` |
| Claim label | Design rationale |
| Evidence level | argument |
| Source queue | primary: `scf`, `rmi`, `benchmaxxing`; supporting: `cognitive_loop_closure`, `talos`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Source loading state | source notes: `scf`, `rmi`, `benchmaxxing`, `cognitive_loop_closure`, `talos`, `moecot`; raw cache: `scf`, `rmi`, `benchmaxxing`, `cognitive_loop_closure`, `talos`; connector/recovery: `moecot` |
| Test state | Planned only; no tests have been run for this chapter in this drafting pass. |

## Drafting guardrail

This chapter is a v0.2 living-book draft. It synthesizes the architecture manifest, the current outline, the source inventory, available source notes, and local raw-source readiness. It does not by itself promote any claim beyond the support state shown in the chapter metadata and Appendix C. Where the chapter uses design reasoning, it is written as architecture argument rather than as reported empirical fact.

## Problem

Recursive improvement requires a safe procedure for replacing components while preserving identity, regression history, and recovery paths.

Stable capability fields define the socket. Replacement defines the transaction that changes what is plugged into it. A system that can improve itself needs a record of the prior implementation, candidate implementation, evidence gates, regressions, residuals, state migration, monitor window, and rollback path.

RMI and Benchmaxxing supply the ratchet discipline: move the frontier while preserving the floor. Cognitive Loop Closure adds lifecycle discipline for tools that emerge from repeated work. Talos adds job, audit, and replay expectations. SCF supplies the identity boundary that the replacement transaction must preserve.

## Why existing approaches are insufficient

Ad hoc upgrades make it hard to know whether a system improved, drifted, lost a regression, or captured its evaluator.

An ad hoc upgrade can pass a visible benchmark and still delete a regression, change a field's semantics, invalidate a state migration, or make rollback impossible. It can also be self-judged: the component being replaced proposes, evaluates, and approves its own successor. That is not recursive improvement; it is evaluator capture with a success story.

Replacement needs transaction semantics because partial success can be worse than failure. The system should know whether it is in precheck, gate, canary, commit, monitor, rollback, quarantine, or retirement.

## Core Claim

[capability-replacement-and-rollback.core, label: Design rationale, support: argument] Capability replacement should be an evidence-gated transaction with preconditions, regression checks, residual records, and rollback.

The claim is architectural: it says how this layer should be shaped if the whole system is expected to remain governed under growth. The current support state is `argument`, so the claim should be read as a design rationale unless a later evidence bundle promotes it. The assigned source set - Stable Capability Fields, Ratcheting Modular Intelligence, Benchmaxxing: The Performance Ratchet, Cognitive Loop Closure, and Talos Protocol - gives the chapter its vocabulary and comparison points, but the public manuscript keeps source-derived support separate from the draft itself.

## Mechanism

The mechanism is a Replacement Transaction.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  A["Prior implementation"] --> B["Replacement proposal"]
  B --> C["Precheck: field identity + authority"]
  C --> D["Qualification gates"]
  D --> E["Regression suite"]
  E --> F{"Pass with rollback ready?"}
  F -- "yes" --> G["Canary / commit"]
  F -- "no" --> H["Reject / quarantine / residual"]
  G --> I["Monitor window"]
  I --> J{"Incident or regression?"}
  J -- "yes" --> K["Rollback"]
  J -- "no" --> L["Promote / retire prior"]
  H --> M["Evidence + changelog"]
  K --> M
  L --> M
```

- Define replacement proposals as artifacts.
- Run qualification, regression, and authority checks.
- Commit only after evidence gates pass and rollback remains available.

The transaction is an artifact before it is a change. It names the field, prior implementation, candidate, gates, evidence refs, regression suite, residual escrow, rollback command or plan, approval record, monitor window, and final decision. Failed gates become residuals rather than disappearing.

Benchmarks are used as evidence surfaces, not permission slips. If a benchmark saturates, becomes gamed, or fails to transfer, that diagnosis belongs in the transaction.

## Interfaces

The interface is a Replacement Transaction.

- SCF defines the field.
- Benchmarks test behavior.
- Evidence and changelog record the transition.

Minimum fields:

- `transaction_id`
- `field_id`
- `prior_implementation`
- `candidate_implementation`
- `precheck_results`
- `qualification_evidence`
- `regression_results`
- `authority_check`
- `residual_escrow`
- `rollback_plan`
- `approval_record`
- `monitor_window`
- `decision`

SCF defines the field. Benchmark ratchets and regression suites test behavior. Evidence and changelog records preserve the transition. Governance approves authority changes and high-impact commits.

## Invariants

The invariants are the conditions that must remain true even when the implementation changes, the source set grows, or later chapters introduce stronger mechanisms.

- No replacement without prior and posterior artifacts.
- Regressions stay attached.
- Rollback metadata is required before promotion.

The transaction must leave enough information to answer a simple question later: what changed, why was it allowed, what got worse, and how could the system return to the prior state?

## Failure modes

The failure model is part of the design, not an afterthought. This layer is expected to fail in ways that can be detected, quarantined, and used to improve the architecture.

- Regression deletion.
- Rollback impossible after deployment.
- Self-judged replacement.

Regression deletion is the classic false improvement: the candidate does better on the frontier while forgetting what the stack already knew. Rollback impossibility turns a bad canary into permanent damage. Self-judged replacement lets a component rewrite the evidence used to approve it. Each failure blocks promotion.

## Minimal implementation

A replacement transaction schema with precheck, gate, commit, monitor, and rollback phases.

The smallest useful implementation is a `replacement_transaction` schema and valid fixture. It should validate required evidence and rollback fields, not claim a real replacement succeeded.

The implementation should be promoted only when its evidence exists. A planned test remains planned; a proof target remains planned; a prototype claim remains unpromoted until the code, command, environment, and result are recorded.

## Codex test plan

| Test | Purpose | Status |
|---|---|---|
| Replacement transaction test | Check that a commit cannot occur without precheck, qualification, regression, approval, and rollback records. | planned; not run |
| Regression preservation test | Check that known regressions remain attached to the candidate decision. | planned; not run |
| Rollback execution dry run | Check that rollback metadata exists before default promotion. | planned; not run |

These tests are acceptance targets for later implementation work. They are not reported results. When a test is implemented, the chapter should link to the command, fixture, environment notes, and result summary, and Appendix E should be regenerated or updated accordingly.

### Formalization hooks

| Tag | Module | Target | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| `lean:replacement.transaction.operational_invariant` | `AsiStackProofs.Replacement` | A replacement commit requires qualification evidence and rollback metadata. | planned |
| `lean:replacement.transaction.failure_blocks_promotion` | `AsiStackProofs.Replacement` | A failed regression blocks promotion of the replacement. | planned |

The table records proof and contract hooks for future work. Planned hooks should become Lean proofs only when the predicate is precise enough to mechanize; otherwise they should become schemas, process contracts, or research-agenda items.

## Source crosswalk

| Source ID | Title | Layer | Planned use | Readiness |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `scf` | Stable Capability Fields | governance_recursive_self_improvement | Use public release v1.0 when available. Stable boundaries, replacement, bounded authority, recoverable evolution. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `rmi` | Ratcheting Modular Intelligence | capability_ratchet | Benchmark pressure, residual escrow, verified modular capability, regression preservation. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `benchmaxxing` | Benchmaxxing: The Performance Ratchet | benchmarks_evidence | Benchmarks as pressure surfaces, saturation -> regression, harder frontier, anti-Goodhart safeguards. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `cognitive_loop_closure` | Cognitive Loop Closure | procedural_memory | Repeated cognition should become procedural memory / verified tools. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `talos` | Talos Protocol | labor_execution_os | AI labor OS. Deterministic cognitive manufacturing, typed jobs, control planes, auditability, tool isolation. | source note available; local raw cache available |
| `moecot` | MoECOT-Agent Architecture Whitepaper | implementation_reference | Concrete implementation evidence: governed low-parameter multi-core runtime, readiness gates, ledgers, replay. | source note available; connector or recovery required |

The crosswalk describes why each source is assigned to the chapter and what is currently available in the repo. A local raw cache or source note makes future mining easier, but this draft still keeps the support state conservative until the specific claim-to-source mapping is recorded.

## Summary

Capability Replacement and Rollback turns improvement into a transaction. It preserves the prior state, candidate evidence, regressions, residuals, approval, monitor window, and rollback path.

The chapter's discipline is floor preservation. The frontier may move only when the existing floor remains visible and recoverable.
